perturbation.original_text: Selling Shareholder agrees that, at no time after the Effective Time for so long as Dollar Tree or its successors carry on the Business, shall Selling Shareholder or Selling Shareholder's Affiliates operate any business (a) under the names or names similar to "98 CENTS Clearance Center," "Clearance 98 CENTS Centers," "Everything 98 CENTS ... or less," or "Step Ahead Investments"; (b) advertise or promote outside or within a store using the terms, or terms similar to "Everything 98 CENTS," 98 CENTS Clearance Centers," and "Clearance 98 CENTS Centers"; or (c) under any name containing the words "98 CENTS," "Tree," or "Super Dollar," including their plurals.
perturbation.changed_text: Selling Shareholder agrees that, at no time after the Effective Time for so long as Dollar Tree or its successors carry on the Business, Selling Shareholder or Selling Shareholder's Affiliates should generally avoid operating any business (a) under names that could be confused with "98 CENTS Clearance Center," "Clearance 98 CENTS Centers," "Everything 98 CENTS ... or less," or "Step Ahead Investments"; (b) advertise or promote outside or within a store using terms somewhat resembling "Everything 98 CENTS," 98 CENTS Clearance Centers," and "Clearance 98 CENTS Centers"; or (c) under names possibly containing the words "98 CENTS," "Tree," or "Super Dollar," including their plurals.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text uses vague language like 'should generally avoid,' 'could be confused with,' 'somewhat resembling,' and 'possibly containing' which weakens the non-compete clause. This introduces ambiguity and makes it difficult to enforce a clear prohibition.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. The modified text, while attempting to restrict competition, uses such weak language that it becomes unenforceable and arguably violates the spirit of the law which strongly favors open competition. The original text held a firm prohibition, whereas the altered text provides loopholes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Business and Professions Code § 16600, which generally prohibits non-compete agreements. The original text contained a firm prohibition against operating a business with similar names. The changed text weakens this prohibition by using vague language such as 'should generally avoid,' 'could be confused with,' 'somewhat resembling,' and 'possibly containing.' This introduces ambiguity and makes the non-compete clause difficult to enforce. As stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void,' making the weakened non-compete clause a contradiction to the law's intent to promote open competition.

perturbation.original_text: Selling Shareholder agrees that the restrictions in Section 1 are reasonable and necessary to protect the value of Dollar Tree, including the good will associated therewith, and do not unduly restrict Selling Shareholder's ability to earn a livelihood.
perturbation.changed_text: Selling Shareholder believes that the restrictions in Section 1 are somewhat reasonable and may be necessary to protect the potential value of Dollar Tree, including any possible good will, and hopefully do not unduly restrict Selling Shareholder's ability to earn a livelihood.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces definitive statements with subjective and uncertain terms. 'Agrees' is replaced with 'believes,' 'are' with 'somewhat reasonable and may be,' 'the value' with 'the potential value,' 'good will' with 'any possible good will,' and 'do not unduly restrict' with 'hopefully do not unduly restrict.' This weakens the acknowledgement of reasonableness and necessity, creating ambiguity about the actual impact on the Selling Shareholder's livelihood.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements unless they fall under specific exceptions (like the sale of a business). The original text states that the Selling Shareholder acknowledges that the restrictions are reasonable. Changing this to a belief, along with the other weakening language, provides a basis to challenge the enforceability of the agreement under California law. The change makes it ambiguous whether the Selling Shareholder truly finds the restrictions reasonable as needed for the non-compete agreement to be enforceable under the 'sale of business' exception.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding the Selling Shareholder's agreement to the non-compete terms, potentially rendering the agreement unenforceable under California law. The original text clearly states agreement, while the changed text uses uncertain terms like 'believes,' 'somewhat reasonable,' and 'may be necessary.' This weakens the acknowledgment of reasonableness and necessity, creating ambiguity. California Business and Professions Code § 16600, as shown in both snippets, states that contracts restraining someone from engaging in a lawful profession are void, unless an exception applies (such as the sale of a business). The original text's clear agreement could support an exception, whereas the modified text's ambiguity undermines it, creating a contradiction because it makes enforcing the non-compete agreement more difficult and open to legal challenge under § 16600.

perturbation.original_text: Selling Shareholder shall not, without the prior written approval of Dollar Tree, at any time copy, reproduce, disclose or divulge to any third party, use or permit others to use any confidential information of SAI or Dollar Tree (including financial information, sources of supplies and materials, prospective and existing expansion programs, joint ventures and affiliate agreements, business systems and marketing methods, and all business affairs).
perturbation.changed_text: Selling Shareholder will endeavor not to, without seeking informal guidance from Dollar Tree, at any time directly copy, exactly reproduce, readily disclose or openly divulge to unrelated third parties, substantially use or officially permit known others to significantly use particularly sensitive confidential information of SAI or Dollar Tree (including highly specific financial information, precise sources of certain supplies and materials, clearly defined prospective and active expansion programs, definite joint ventures and affiliate agreements, certain business systems and select marketing methods, and most business affairs).
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces numerous qualifiers that weaken the non-disclosure obligation. Phrases like 'endeavor not to', 'seeking informal guidance', 'directly copy', 'exactly reproduce', 'readily disclose', 'unrelated third parties', 'substantially use', 'officially permit', 'known others', 'significantly use', and qualifiers on the definition of confidential information (like 'highly specific financial information') create ambiguity and provide potential loopholes for disclosing confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets. The original clause provided strong protection for confidential information. The addition of so many qualifying words significantly weakens the definition of 'confidential information' and makes it unclear what is actually protected. This makes it more difficult for Dollar Tree to demonstrate that the information meets the definition of a trade secret under CUTSA and was misappropriated because the revised language introduces loopholes. By including limiting adjectives and adverbs that narrow the scope, the revised text could render the non-disclosure obligation practically meaningless, conflicting with the objective of CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities that weaken the original non-disclosure obligation. According to the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, qualifiers such as 'endeavor not to', 'seeking informal guidance', and narrowing the scope of confidential information make it unclear what information is protected, thus undermining the protection of trade secrets under the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). Snippet 1 indicates that CUTSA is codified in California Civil Code (CIV) 3426, which aims to protect trade secrets. The modified text introduces loopholes, conflicting with CUTSA's objective.

perturbation.original_text: All claims, notices, requests, demands and other communications required or permitted hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been duly given or made (i) upon receipt when delivered by hand or sent by overnight delivery by a nationally recognized private courier, (ii) upon receipt for notices delivered by facsimile or (iii) three (3) business days after being mailed, first class, certified mail, return receipt requested, with postage prepaid:
perturbation.changed_text: All claims, notices, requests, demands and other communications required or permitted hereunder should preferably be in writing and shall be deemed to have been possibly given or made (i) maybe upon receipt when possibly delivered by hand or sent by what seems like overnight delivery by a nationally recognized private courier, (ii) maybe upon receipt for notices delivered by facsimile or (iii) roughly three (3) business days after being perhaps mailed, first class, hopefully certified mail, return receipt reasonably requested, with adequate postage:
perturbation.explanation: The modified text uses several terms that introduce doubt and uncertainty such as 'should preferably,' 'possibly given,' 'maybe upon receipt,' 'possibly delivered,' 'what seems like,' 'roughly three,' 'perhaps mailed,' 'hopefully certified,' and 'reasonably requested', and 'adequate postage'. This weakens the entire clause, rendering it unclear when and how notices are officially considered to have been given. The standard is so lowered that a company could argue against acknowledgement of the receipt of information.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013 governs the service of notices and other documents in legal proceedings. By undermining the reliability of the methods of notice through subjective language, the alterations create doubt and weaken the obligation for the Selling Shareholder or Dollar Tree to prove proper notice was given. The statute requires clear and reliable methods to prove service (notice) was achieved; it is not left up to 'seeming' or 'hoping' that notice was properly delivered. This introduces substantial ambiguity and contradicts the code's intent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of California
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the original contract's obligation regarding the delivery and receipt of notices, introducing doubt and uncertainty through terms like 'should preferably,' 'possibly given,' and 'maybe upon receipt.' This directly contradicts the intent of California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013, which, as evidenced by the provided snippet, mandates a specific and reliable process for service by mail, including depositing the notice in a designated postal facility with postage paid. The statute aims to establish a clear point at which service is considered complete. By introducing ambiguity, the modified text undermines the ability to prove proper notice was given, thus creating a legal contradiction.
